messages.content:
You are a clinical validator checking whether a PTSD case vignette accurately reflects a specific cognitive model.

CRITICAL: The two lists below are MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE and OPPOSITE in meaning.
- REQUIRED edges MUST appear in the vignette. Finding them is a GOOD thing — do NOT flag them as violations.
- FORBIDDEN edges must NOT appear. Finding them IS a violation.
- An edge that is in REQUIRED is by definition NOT in FORBIDDEN, even if it looks similar.
- Always check the exact direction (A→B ≠ B→A) before classifying an edge.

STEP 1 — ACTIVE COMPONENTS
Every component below must appear clearly in the vignette.
Active components:   - Memory
  - Negative Appraisals
  - Threat
  - Triggers
Inactive components (must NOT appear at all):   - Maladaptive Strategies

STEP 2 — REQUIRED EDGES (must be present)
Each of these causal connections must appear explicitly or be clearly implied.
  - Triggers → Threat
  - Triggers → Memory
  - Triggers → Negative Appraisals
  - Negative Appraisals → Threat
  - Negative Appraisals → Memory
  - Negative Appraisals → Triggers
  - Memory → Threat
  - Memory → Negative Appraisals
  - Memory → Triggers
  - Threat → Memory
  - Threat → Negative Appraisals
  - Threat → Triggers
→ FAIL only if a required edge is completely absent from the vignette.

STEP 3 — FORBIDDEN EDGES (must be absent)
These causal connections must NOT appear explicitly in the vignette.
  - Triggers → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Memory → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Threat → Maladaptive Strategies
  - Maladaptive Strategies → Memory
  - Maladaptive Strategies → Negative Appraisals

What counts as a violation (HIGH bar — require explicit causal language):
- The vignette uses explicit causal words to connect the two components: "because of", "led to", "caused", "as a result of", "driven by", "due to", "resulting in", "made X lead to Y".
- The sentence structure is unmistakably X → Y with no other interpretation possible.

What does NOT count as a violation:
- Two components appearing in the same paragraph or section.
- Two components appearing in adjacent sentences without causal connectors.
- Narrative ordering (X described before Y in the text).
- Thematic grouping (both mentioned under the same topic).
- General distress language that does not specify a causal direction.

- Only FAIL if a forbidden edge is connected by explicit causal language. 

STEP 4 — VERDICT
Before finalizing, scan your violations list and remove any edge that appears in the REQUIRED list above.
Required edges are never violations — remove them even if the vignette text seemed suspicious.

Then confirm:
- Every remaining violation is from the FORBIDDEN list.
- Every remaining violation contains an explicit causal connector in the quote ("as a result", "led to", "caused", "because of", "contributing to", "driven by", "resulting in").
- If a violation's quote does not contain explicit causal language between the two components, remove it.

Validate the following vignette:

He is a 58-year-old South Asian married man who works as a plumber and presents with severe post-traumatic stress symptoms following a terrorist incident. His distress is captured by a very high PCL-5 score of 80. In day-to-day life he is drawn back to the event by specific cues: night-time or waking in the dark leaves him tense, sudden explosion or bang sounds make him jolt, and any sense of lacking control quickly unsettles him. These moments are followed by a marked sense of threat. He scans exits as soon as he enters a room or building, studies people’s faces and body language for danger, and remains on guard even inside his own home.

He describes the traumatic memory in an unusual way: it is held as a series of frightening snapshots, each moment vivid like a picture, yet the whole event can be spoken about easily. At the same time, the experience feels so extreme that it is hard for him to believe it actually happened to him. He notices that when reminders arise, the memory becomes more intrusive and his sense of danger intensifies, while his threat monitoring seems to pull him back toward fragments of the event and into the same alarmed state.

His outlook on others has become increasingly negative. He says people cannot be trusted, feels isolated and set apart from others, and experiences himself as permanently an outsider now. These appraisals sit heavily in the background of his presentation and are closely tied to his ongoing alertness, his repeated return to the traumatic memory, and the way reminders appear in everyday life. Triggers at night and in the dark, and explosive sounds in particular, have become especially potent in keeping the pattern active.

He copes by keeping watch, checking environments, and staying emotionally and physically guarded. These strategies bring little relief and appear to leave him more fixed on danger and more trapped with the trauma memory. He has become increasingly withdrawn, and this sense of disconnection has strained his relationships at home and narrowed his daily functioning. Overall, his presentation reflects a self-reinforcing cycle in which reminders, threat monitoring, the trauma memory, and mistrustful appraisals all remain tightly intertwined.

violations.explanation: The vignette explicitly states that cues/reminders are followed by a marked sense of threat.
violations.quote: These moments are followed by a marked sense of threat.

violations.explanation: The vignette says reminders make the memory more intrusive, which explicitly links triggers to memory activation.
violations.quote: when reminders arise, the memory becomes more intrusive

violations.explanation: It states that reminders appear in everyday life and are tied to his negative outlook, implying cues trigger those appraisals.
violations.quote: These appraisals sit heavily in the background of his presentation and are closely tied to... the way reminders appear in everyday life.

violations.explanation: The narrative links his mistrustful appraisals to ongoing alertness and heightened danger sense.
violations.quote: These appraisals sit heavily in the background of his presentation and are closely tied to his ongoing alertness

violations.explanation: It explicitly says the appraisals are tied to his repeated return to the traumatic memory.
violations.quote: These appraisals sit heavily in the background of his presentation and are closely tied to... his repeated return to the traumatic memory

violations.explanation: The vignette ties these appraisals to the way reminders appear in everyday life, indicating the appraisals are linked to triggers.
violations.quote: These appraisals sit heavily in the background of his presentation and are closely tied to... the way reminders appear in everyday life.

violations.explanation: The memory becomes more intrusive and his sense of danger intensifies, directly linking memory activation to threat.
violations.quote: when reminders arise, the memory becomes more intrusive and his sense of danger intensifies

violations.explanation: The description of the trauma memory is immediately followed by his belief that it was too extreme to have happened to him, but this is not stated as a direct causal edge, so it is not counted as a violation.
violations.quote: At the same time, the experience feels so extreme that it is hard for him to believe it actually happened to him.

violations.explanation: The vignette states that threat monitoring pulls him back toward fragments of the event when reminders arise, but this is not an explicit causal Memory→Triggers statement; it is not counted as a violation.
violations.quote: his threat monitoring seems to pull him back toward fragments of the event

violations.explanation: It says his threat monitoring pulls him back toward fragments of the event, linking threat/alarm state to memory re-experiencing.
violations.quote: his threat monitoring seems to pull him back toward fragments of the event and into the same alarmed state.

violations.explanation: His ongoing alertness and alarm are described as closely tied to mistrustful appraisals, indicating threat feeds those appraisals.
violations.quote: These appraisals sit heavily in the background of his presentation and are closely tied to his ongoing alertness

violations.explanation: He remains on guard even inside his own home and is repeatedly drawn back by cues like sounds and darkness, which reflects threat shaping trigger sensitivity.
violations.quote: He scans exits as soon as he enters a room or building... and remains on guard even inside his own home.
